Jordan, much of the development literature talks about creating goals or a set of goals. To you, what's the difference between the vision and the goals? And how do you use the set of goals to achieve that vision? Okay, well, goals to me are your stopping points along the way that tell you whether or not you're moving in the right direction towards your vision, or you're essentially moving in the wrong direction towards your vision. So you have your vision, which is on top of everything. It's like this higher level function. It's this higher level target that you have, which really represents you. It encapsulates your beliefs, your desires, your wants, your hope for your future at this very high level. And it's not really a date on it. It's sort of just where you want to end up in your life, as opposed to a goal, which has a start date and an end date. I set a goal today, and it should be achieved by June 31, 2013. That's a goal. It has an end date. A vision doesn't. That's number one. Number two, I don't become emotionally invested in my goals, but I do become emotionally invested in my vision. To me, if I think about my vision and I read my vision to myself, if I don't get to that spot where I am almost, I feel my pulse beating through my wrists and the hairs in the back of my neck stand out, I don't get to that point of excitability, and that point where I feel empowered and motivated. And my vision is not really the vision that I need to get what I want in life. I need to redo that vision to get me to that sort of point to where I'm excited and pumped and motivated. When I look at my goals, I don't get motivated like that. I'm not looking to get motivated. When I look at my goals, I'm looking for strategy of maybe managing time and how much effort, essentially what people don't need to meet to help me achieve this goal. What processes do I have to learn if there are any processes? What bodies of knowledge do I need to familiarize myself with to achieve a goal? So a goal is almost a logical point that you're moving towards, while the vision's more of an emotional point you're moving towards. And I think that the two together are incredibly powerful. So I'm not, you know, back in the day of, you know, we had this amazing, actually it was a recording back then in the 60s, it was by a man named Earl Nightingale, and it was called The Stranger's Secret. And basically at the heart of the book was this idea, which back then was revolutionary, is that you become what you think about, that you can visualize where you want to go and you can essentially manifest that in your life. Back then that was groundbreaking. And at the heart of Earl Nightingale's formula was this sort of goal-setting mentality to set goals, right, and then to move towards those goals and have them as your targets. And he used this study, which turned out to not be true, by the way, and they had taken some study at Yale University and the 5% of the people at Yale in the graduating class of 1940 who set goals ended up making more money than the other 95% of Yale who didn't set goals. It turned out not to be true, but that was a famous study for how it turned out to be false. The point was this though, and it is true, is that people who do set goals will tend to be much more successful and people who don't set goals strictly because a goal has a target and, you know, it's very hard to almost hit something that you can't see. You know, you're not a target in your life, good luck trying to get somewhere. You see, you know, you've got something like a random preacher. What happened was is that in the 1960s, that was earth shattering stuff. You know, people would say, I'm goal oriented, you know, I'm going to set goals and it really worked. Over the years, it became bastardized. This idea of goal setting became so commonplace that, you know, everyone became goal oriented. And, you know, if you look at anyone's resume, I'm a goal oriented individual, blah, blah, blah, and it means nothing. You know, you know, how many times have we all set goals and not achieved them? Everyone's raised their hand. We almost become conditioned not to achieve our goals. So the power that the strangest secret used to have, which was immense and based on goals, has now lost its power because we have essentially bastardized the word goals. Now it's about vision. It's transcended to that next level up, which means you have to have your vision. That's what empowers you. The goals now are the stopping point, essentially the map along the way, the scientist as I'm heading towards the destination, right? Whether I achieve it or not, it's not going to dictate whether I'm after your set in life. It just tells me if I'm heading in the right direction. If I'm heading towards my vision, I'll feel some pleasure out of it. If I feel I'm heading away from my vision, I'll feel distressed and that causes me to want to work harder and to figure out a new strategy. But I'm always constantly setting goals, abandoning goals that haven't worked, resetting one, and if a goal works, I move to the next goal. But the vision is that sort of that shining light that stands above it all. John, can you go through the specific steps that someone would need to go through to set their goals? Sure. I mean, very simple. To me, goal setting is a mathematical equation and it starts like this. Set a goal, number one. Number two, put a date on it. Every goal has to have a date. Then you have short-term goals, which could be in, you know, it could be every day you could have goals. End of day goals, right? That you could make them or not make them by the end of the day, then you discard them and set new goals the next day. So those are ultra short-term goals. But you have short-term goals that are like, you know, one-month goals. You have three-month goals. You have intermediate goals that are like maybe a year and you have long-term goals that are three years. And then, you know, past, you know, two, three years, you start getting into sort of more to me, the vision aspect of things, right? But those are the various goals. They have dates on them. Step three. So number one, set a goal. Two, put a date on it. Step three, identify the people, groups, and organizations that you need to align yourself with to achieve your goal. In other words, in any goal out there, typically you're not going to do it in a vacuum. There's people you need to know, organizations you might need to join, employees you need to hire, whatever it might be, identify all those peoples, those groups, those organizations that you need to have access to. Step four, learn what you need to know. Figure out, is there stuff I got to learn? And if there is, educate yourself. Find out what you're hire someone that essentially knows what you need to know. So, again, let's go through it. Step one, set a goal. Step two, put a date on it. Step three, identify the peoples, groups, and organizations that you need to align yourself with. Step four, learn what you need to know. Set by develop a plan, meaning the best plan available based on the information you currently have. There are no perfect plans. You don't want to be one of these people that develops a plan forever. I'm still on the planning stage. When I'm done planning, then I'll get started. And there is no perfect plan because the plan that you end up developing will probably change four or five times before you actually end up getting what you want. So, but you have to have a plan. So, you don't have the best plan available based on the information you currently have about the market, about business itself, and your experience. Step six, take massive action. Not a little bit of action. Take massive action towards that goal. And go out and do what you got to do. Step seven, do what's called have visual acuity. Analyze your results. See what's working. See what's not working. Anytime we take actions towards a goal, we'll find that some of those actions are actually getting us what we want. And in fact, probably more of those actions in the beginning aren't getting us what we want. So, you want to actually, you know, analyze your results, see what's working, what's not, and change your approach, which is step eight is one step at a time, change your approach. So, we don't want to change everything at once, but if it's not working, you change one thing, see if it's working again, and you keep running that loop, you know, change your approach, see what's working, change your approach. How many times do you change your approach until you get what you want until you see yourself moving in the right direction? Now, there is one caveat to that is that, you know, obviously you don't want to be a moron who's bagging his head against the wall, his or her head against the wall forever. So, for a certain amount of times of change your approach, we're still not getting what you want in life. You have to say, hmm, maybe there's something wrong with the actual goal. Maybe the goal was flawed. Maybe I'm missing some of the strategies myself. So, it doesn't occur in a vacuum, you know, good after a certain point in time, you know, you don't want to get, and by the way, this is the danger of becoming emotionally invested in your goals. Because if you become so emotionally invested in your goal and you're not achieving it, you'll become irrational, you'll irrationally be chasing after your goal. You'll keep changing your approach and rather than saying, you know, maybe you should abandon the goals, maybe it's a bad business, maybe it's a bad idea altogether. So that's why you want to keep rational instead of becoming irrational and getting emotionally attached to the goal. So those are the steps. Let's go through one more time. Step one, set a goal. Step two, put a date on it. Step three, identify the people's groups and organizations you need to alarm yourself with. Step four, learn what you've got to learn. Know what you've got to know. Step five, develop a plan of attack. Step six, take massive action, right? Step seven, analyze your results. And lastly, see what's working, what's not, change your approach. But simple as that. Although it's been proven that 90% of people don't follow through on their goals. So what's the difference here? What, how do you have such a higher level of success with people following through and achieving their goals using the straight line system? Number one, I'm not, I'm not sure you got that number of 90% for it. It probably sounds pretty accurate. So let's just assume that that is accurate. I would say the problem is, for the most part, is that most people who do set goals don't do it that way. They don't, they don't actually set goals, put dates on them, figure out who they got to know to achieve the goal, what they got to learn to achieve the goal. They don't develop a great plan. Oh, they've spent too much time on the plan. They try to develop the perfect plan when there is none. And they don't have that sensory acuity to keep looking at it and changing their approach and essentially cultivating the art of flexibility, learning how to be flexible when you take action. So you see what's working and what's not. And don't say, you know, no, I'm going to keep doing this and banging my head against the wall whether I like it or not, because that's my plan. My plan is a good plan. You know, so that's, I think, a lot of the reason why people don't achieve goals. And on top of that, though, I think even more important, at least equally as important, is because they don't have the vision on top of their goal. So the goal doesn't have the right meaning. They're not using a goal for what it is. In other words, a goal is not so much about the achievement of it. The goal is just essentially, it's almost like a directional mechanism to make sure you're moving in the right direction. It's not so much about the achievement of the goal. Yeah, of course, you'd rather achieve than not achieve. But at the end of the day, your most important thing is that you're moving towards your vision. So I say that for the people who are, you know, the bulk of people who are setting goals and not achieving them, A, you're probably engaged in flawed goal setting, B, and this is a big one, you're probably a bit light on the strategy of the whole thing. In other words, the beauty of the straight line is that it is a technique, a technology that allows you to achieve. You know, go out there and set a goal to become a top producer and you don't know how to become a world-class close. Well, good luck being a top. You can want to be a top producer your whole life. If you don't have the strategy to do it, you're going to be in trouble. So the straight line to me in terms of you're in sales and really in business in any way, shape, or form, the straight line is that almost that X factor, it's that really that almost, let's say the equalizer to people who are just getting started and it allows you to achieve your goals more than not achieve your goals. And whether you do or not is really not that important as long as above those goals, you have a vision that keeps you moving in the right direction. So those two combined, I think are really the core, those three combined, the three core reasons that people don't achieve. A, they just engage in flawed goal setting. B, they lack the strategy to get what they want in life. They don't know how to sell and end up becoming closer. They don't have a straight line tactic to close. Everyone is closeable. And three, they don't have the vision on top of that. Keep them inspired. And with that comes their why and everything we've already talked about, which essentially keeps you moving in the direction you need to move in when the barriers and obstacles present themselves as they always do. And instead of you getting negative and feeling disempowered, that why, that vision, it allows you to essentially be irresistible, irresistible force moving towards your goal and breaking down the barriers in your way. That's simple. What are some of the things in people's lives that could get in the way potentially of them achieving their vision and their goals? And what advice do you have to deal and manage with those things? Certainly number one, you know, I guess, okay, let's take it to the most basic level. Number one is your physical health. Okay, you know, you know, my body's my temple. You know, I shouldn't say it because I kind of polluted the temple pretty bad, but I ended up okay. I got lucky, right? But the point is, is that there's a certain amount of just physical energy that you need in life to go out and do what you want. So, you know, if you're out there piling like a rock star every single night and they wonder why I can't get what I want the next day, well, you know, maybe you need to take a good look at that, you know, you know, the health is our health is something that we never really think about till we don't have it anymore. But at the heart of wall success is the ability to have energy, the energy to go out and work hard and takes hard work. So that's number one, you know, when I was in the seminar business and I didn't do it for very long, I just didn't, I didn't, didn't feed my spirit to go and give seminars like that. I do trainings. It's very different what I do. But one of the things that almost held me back from being really great at seminars with people like this average people that are out there is that I go out there and I was saying, you got to work really, really hard. Well, the average seminar guy says, just take this little box here. It's magical. Give me two grand in the stake. And when you're under where plugged the box and you'll be rich forever. Now, when I see that, I run the other way. I tell people, you got to work really, really hard and you do. There are no shortcuts to hard work. Okay. So then that comes from what? Having the energy, the physical, you know, embodiment of the spirit to go out there and work really hard. That's number one. People aren't going to do the work. Now on the energy level, they just, they're physically ill or out of shape and they can't go out and achieve that's number one. Number two, another reason I think people don't achieve their goals or, you know, really can't get what they want in life is because they, again, they lack a skill set. They're, they're really not, they're not there on the skill level. They don't understand that there's a lot of work that you need to do in drilling and perfecting your, your honing your craft, so to speak. You know, an actor doesn't go out to Hollywood and try to become a star without taking any acting lessons. They take years and years of classes and lessons and try to improve and hone their skill. Right. So if you're not, if you're not dedicated to becoming great at what you do, then shame on you. You know, the, in fact, the reason for this program, these tapes, these, these modules is to actually serve as essentially the food that you need to consume, the knowledge that you need to consume as a salesperson. So you're great at it. I mean, that's it. So I think that's another major reason what, what that, you know, you have to have that sort of commitment to learning that commitment to gain knowledge. Okay. Without that, it's very, very hard to achieve. Um, I think another major sort of pitfall for people who are out there is that they might have, they have the energy, they have the knowledge, but they have not gotten to the point where they understand the importance of their day to day emotional state, their emotional cripples. They feel great one day, they feel terrible another day. So they have the strategy. They have the personal power to go do things, but they're like sea source. They feel great two days, lousy three days. So essentially they're only the bets they can be half the time or less than half the time. Right. That holds so many people back from achieving success. So, you know, one of the things that we could also go over in these modules is, you know, learning how to manage your own state to essentially make sure that you do show up each day and be the best you can possibly when it comes to work and to sales. Right. And then lastly, I'd say the one last thing is by your own beliefs that you hold about yourself. If you have, if you're saddled with limiting beliefs, okay, it tends to really, really hold you back from achieving your goals. So you might have everything else in place, but you have some deep seated belief that maybe I'm not meant for greatness or maybe sales is evil or maybe I'm not good with money or maybe I wasn't meant to be, you know, a leader of my company. And those limiting beliefs, if you have those beliefs, you will not, almost you're almost constitutionally incapable of exceeding the level of your lowest limiting belief. So it's that, that, but you could have everything else. Now one belief that holds you back knocks you out of the box and truly succeeding. So again, which is why in these modules, we also do do some work on, on belief systems and making sure that you have, if you have any limiting beliefs, we route them out to it, essentially free you up to achieve. And lastly, standards, your personal sense, you know, are you set up the excellence? Is your, do you demand excellence from yourself? You could have everything that's set up perfectly, but your version of success is anything to make 80 grand a year. Well, if that's the case, you know, or 40, 40 grand years, doesn't matter what it is. If that's the case, that's all you're going to make. You know, you, you set, you end up getting in life what you want, what your standard is. If your standard is very low, you know, good luck trying to achieve greatness in your life. So that's a lot of intergame stuff, you know, because again, you know, we have that inner world and outer world, if strategy is intact and our goal setting is being done correctly, if we're not achieving walls, pretty much a good bet that it's inner world stuff that's holding people back. Jordan, thank you so much. I think you've given us a really clear insight into what it means to create a solid vision to give us the direction of where we need to go and how to develop and attain those specific goals to get there. Just a final question. What's your personal vision for the future now? Where do you see yourself in the next few years? You know, it's pretty interesting time for me because of, you know, so much going on right now and I have a movie coming out about my life, right, with Leonardo DiCaprio is playing me. I'm still alive and dead yet. I'm already got Leo playing me, right? So it's pretty interesting. You know, I, in a sense, I've been struggling. It's a good question over the last, you know, three to six months of really saving myself what's next for me because I've achieved pretty much everything I've wanted in my life right now at this point. You know, I was coming from such a bad spot when I lost everything and then I made all my money back and, you know, started empowering lots of people around the world. And I started just feeling so good that it would be easy for me to fall into the trap of saying I just want more of the same. Truth is, I think that I am better than that for myself. I think I owe myself more. I think I owe the world more. I think I have, I certainly have some, some great gifts I was given from God or nature, whatever they are, the ability to inspire people, to empower people. And I think that it would be unfair of me to not sort of share that at a higher level. You know, in fact, one of the reasons why I'm doing this, this product here and, you know, this is corporate around the world, you know, the corporate world is because I couldn't possibly service all the people around the world. And I'm one person. I can't go training, you know, five million people, right? So this product itself allows me to leverage myself to get the message out there, to be able to teach people how to, you know, become closers, how to become ethical closers and, you know, how to become visionaries in their own life and how to inspire themselves and inspire other people, right? But I think that's just, you know, one element of my life. I think the next great element for me is to really expand on my charity work that I do. I've done a lot of stuff in Africa right now with impoverished kids. And I actually go in there and use these same lessons and these same strategies and skill sets to, you know, give hope sometimes to what's the hope lesson and hopefully inspire these kids and make a change. And at least some of them have been helped break the cycle of poverty. So, you know, for me, you know, I travel so much around the world. I'm constantly jet lagged and I always find that matter how jet lag I am. When I find myself in a township working with kids, I feel amazing. It feeds my spirit at the highest level. So I think there's a lot more of that in my future. And I think I really am at that point right now, where probably, you know, we'll have to sort of re-clarify my own vision for the future. I could only imagine what the future holds to me when this movie comes out. I just don't even know what it's going to be like. So, you know, at this point, I would say I'm probably got another three to six months on autopilot where I'm at now or because, you know, I'm living my vision. And then I think, you know, you know, I'm always searching around and saying, you know, what's the next conquest? You know, what's the next thing? I don't think I'll, you know, do a Richard Branson and try to blast off into space and try to, you know, put a colony on the moon. I love Richard. He's a friend of mine. I think my future probably lies on the planet still, you know, but maybe in some of the continents like Africa and, you know, the places around the world where the kids are really, really sorely lacking in the sort of skill sets I know that I can impart on them that will empower them and really help these communities break the cycles of poverty and really make a change. And that to me would be, you know, huge success and a great sort of finale to my life because I've kind of pretty much lived 10 or 12 lives that I think I'm going to be able to speak to. And that would be a really great last life would be to really use the stuff that I once kind of misused and used to get rich myself and turn people into closers, but really wasn't using ethically. And now to take that same stuff and use it at the highest possible ethical level, that to me would be true success in my life. So that's probably the wrong going. Well, we're all excited to see the results of that in six months down the line. So Jordan Belfort, thank you very much. Thank you. So everyone, guys, that's our interview here. And I think that Victoria did an amazing job. And I, you know, hopefully I was able to use this sort of Q&A to dig much deeper into the sort of the aspect of vision focus. And I could have, I just was speaking to you cold in front of a Cambridge I hate doing. You'll notice that in this module, there's going to be some live footage that's been spliced in from an event I dig. I thought it was very important for you to see me on stage in a controlled environment, did a specific event just to have footage for this product here. Because I think it's crucial for you to see the different levels of energy that I'm able to move to when there's people in front of me, because you're going to find the same thing for yourself in terms of, you know, it's one thing to drill back and forth with the person sitting in front of you when you're practicing this, your straight line tactics. And there's another thing to actually get out into the field and to be doing it in the real world. So I think you'll really enjoy seeing me on stage and seeing some of these lessons that I actually have, you know, said to Victoria here and actually see me do them now in front of a live audience. And I think it will really take you up just one more level and hopefully you'll be able to use this, go through your workbook, do the lessons that are in the workbook and really take these strategies and internalize them at the highest level for yourself. You know, this is, you know, this intergame stuff, vision focus, it's the gift you give to yourself. You know, this is for you. It's going to allow you to become a more effective person, to become a more empowered person, to become a person that's almost a beacon of light, so to speak in your own family and to everyone around you. Because when you see someone who's a true visionary and you're around them, you almost feel empowered. Like it rubs off on other people. You become a positive force in everyone's life that's around you. So that's the gift you give to yourself and the gift that you give to the people that you love. So take this module and, you know, really, you know, commit it to your heart, commit it to your mind and make it a part of who you are. And besides seeing massive results in the sales business and your job, it will definitely propel you there. You'll also see amazing results in every other aspect of your life as well. So I hope you enjoyed the module. Do the workbook and I will see you on the next module. Take care.
